h(n)=0 is admissible (never overestimates). A* is a generalisation that can be more efficient when a good heuristic is available. Dijkstra explores all directions equally; A* focuses search toward the goal

questions.title: Mutable state in OCaml
questions.prompt: OCaml supports mutable state via references. What is a reference? Give the types of ref, !, and :=. Write a counter that can be incremented and read.

markscheme.text: Reference: a mutable cell containing a value. ref : 'a -> 'a ref; (!) : 'a ref -> 'a; (:=) : 'a ref -> 'a -> unit
Counter: let count = ref 0; increment: count := !count + 1; read: !count
The ! operator dereferences (reads the current value); := assigns a new value. Not to be confused with boolean not or field access

questions.title: Loops in OCaml
questions.prompt: OCaml provides for and while loops. Write the following using loops (not recursion): (i) sum the integers from 1 to n; (ii) compute n! imperatively. State when you would prefer loops over recursion in OCaml.

markscheme.text: (i) let sum_to n = let s = ref 0 in for i = 1 to n do s := !s + i done; !s
(ii) let fact n = let r = ref 1 in for i = 2 to n do r := !r * i done; !r
Prefer loops: when algorithm is naturally iterative (e.g. scanning an array); when stack overflow is a concern for large n with non-tail recursion; when interfacing with imperative data structures

questions.title: Mutable arrays and in-place algorithms
questions.prompt: In OCaml, arrays are mutable. Write an in-place insertion sort using OCaml arrays. State the type of your function. Compare its memory usage to the functional list-based insertion sort.
questions.hint: Array element access: arr.(i); assignment: arr.(i) <- v.

markscheme.text: Type: int array -> unit (sorts in-place, returns unit)
Correct outer loop: for j = 1 to n-1; inner: extract arr.(j), shift elements right while they're larger, insert
Memory: O(1) extra space (in-place). Functional list isort: O(n) extra space (creates new list nodes). Array version preferred for large data sets where memory is tight

questions.title: Exceptions and error handling
questions.prompt:
(i) Explain OCaml's exception mechanism. How do you define, raise, and catch an exception?
(ii) Compare exceptions to the option type for error handling — when would you use each?
(iii) Implement a safe integer division function using both approaches (exception-based and option-based) and discuss the trade-offs.

markscheme.text: Define: exception MyError of string. Raise: raise (MyError "message"). Catch: try ... with MyError msg -> ...

Option type: explicit error handling in types; forces caller to handle None; type-safe; no overhead for success path. Exceptions: errors are implicit in types; can propagate automatically; lightweight for exceptional cases; can lead to uncaught exceptions

Exception version: let div x y = if y=0 then raise Division_by_zero else x/y. Option version: let safe_div x y = if y=0 then None else Some (x/y). Option is preferred in functional style; exceptions for truly unexpected errors

questions.title: Imperative vs functional: benchmarking and semantics
questions.prompt: This question concerns the relationship between imperative and functional programming in OCaml.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Implement matrix multiplication in OCaml using mutable arrays. Give the time complexity. Compare memory usage to a purely functional implementation.

markscheme.text: Triple nested loop: create n×n result array; for each (i,j), compute sum k of A[i][k]*B[k][j]. let matmul a b n = let c = Array.make_matrix n n 0 in for i=0 to n-1 do for j=0 to n-1 do for k=0 to n-1 do c.(i).(j) <- c.(i).(j) + a.(i).(k) * b.(k).(j) done done done; c
Time O(n³), space O(n²) for result. Functional: same complexity but creates n² new values; imperative in-place updates are cache-friendlier

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: What is referential transparency? Give an example showing that OCaml references break it. Why does this matter for equational reasoning?

markscheme.text: Referential transparency: replacing any expression with its value doesn't change program behaviour. Pure functional expressions are referentially transparent
Counterexample: let x = ref 0 in let f () = (incr x; !x) in f () + f () ≠ 2 * f () because f has a side effect. The two calls return different values
Equational reasoning fails: can't substitute equal things for equal things freely. Testing becomes harder (order-dependent). Concurrent access introduces races

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Describe the concept of a closure with mutable state (a 'stateful closure'). Write an OCaml function make_counter : unit -> (unit -> int) that returns a new counter each time it's called, with independent state.

markscheme.text: let make_counter () = let n = ref 0 in fun () -> incr n; !n
Each call to make_counter creates a fresh ref n; the returned closure captures this specific ref. Different counters have independent state
let c1 = make_counter () and c2 = make_counter () in c1(); c1(); c2() gives 1, 2, 1 — they are independent

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Explain the difference between shallow and deep copy for OCaml data structures. When is each needed? Give an example using arrays.

markscheme.text: Shallow copy: copies the top-level structure; nested mutable references are shared. Deep copy: recursively copies all mutable sub-structures
OCaml Array.copy a: shallow copy — new array, but if elements are mutable (e.g. int ref array), the refs inside are shared

Deep copy needed when: you want full independence between original and copy (modifying one's nested structure should not affect the other). Example: let deep_copy a = Array.init (Array.length a) (fun i -> ref !(a.(i))) for int ref array

questions.title: Tripos-style: imperative programming and correctness
questions.prompt: This question concerns imperative programming in OCaml.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Write an imperative OCaml function array_max : int array -> int that returns the maximum element. State a loop invariant and use it to argue correctness.

markscheme.text: let array_max a = if Array.length a = 0 then raise (Invalid_argument "empty") else let m = ref a.(0) in for i=1 to Array.length a - 1 do if a.(i) > !m then m := a.(i) done; !m
Loop invariant: after iteration i, !m = max(a[0], ..., a[i])
Correctness: invariant holds initially (i=0: m=a[0]). Maintained: if a[i+1] > m, update m; otherwise m remains the max. At termination (i=n-1): m = max of all elements

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Implement binary search on a sorted integer array in OCaml imperatively (using a while loop and references). Prove correctness using a loop invariant.

markscheme.text: Correct imperative binary search: lo, hi references; while lo ≤ hi, compute mid, compare a[mid] with target, update lo or hi or return
Loop invariant: if target is in the array, it is in a[lo..hi]
Correctness: invariant holds initially (full array). Each step halves the search space while maintaining invariant. Termination: lo > hi → target not in array

parts.label: c
parts.prompt:
The following OCaml code has a bug. Identify and fix it.
let bubble_sort a =
  let n = Array.length a in
  for i = 0 to n-1 do
    for j = 0 to n-1 do
      if a.(j) > a.(j+1) then begin
        let tmp = a.(j) in
        a.(j) <- a.(j+1);
        a.(j+1) <- tmp
      end
    done
  done

markscheme.text: Bug: inner loop goes to n-1 but accesses a.(j+1) = a.(n) which is out of bounds
Fix: change inner loop to for j = 0 to n-2 do
Additional improvement: outer loop should only go to n-2; or stop early if no swap was made in a pass (optimised bubble sort)

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: In OCaml, Array.sort sorts an array in-place. Its specification says it is 'not guaranteed stable'. Write a test that demonstrates the difference between a stable and unstable sort on an array of pairs, and explain what you observe.

markscheme.text: Test: let a = [|(2,'b'); (1,'a'); (2,'a')|] in Array.sort (fun (i,_) (j,_) -> compare i j) a. Stable sort would give [|(1,'a');(2,'b');(2,'a')|] (preserving input order for equal keys). Unstable might give [|(1,'a');(2,'a');(2,'b')|]
OCaml's Array.sort may rearrange the two (2,_) elements in either order. The test reveals this by checking whether 'b' or 'a' comes first among the 2s
For guaranteed stability: use Array.stable_sort, which guarantees preserving relative order of equal elements; slightly higher constant factor but same O(n log n) complexity
